coredata.dc:title: Three-dimensional porous reduced graphene oxide decorated with carbon quantum dots and platinum nanoparticles for highly selective determination of azo dye compound tartrazine
coredata.dc:creator: Mehmandoust, Mohammad
Erk, Nevin
Karaman, Onur
Karimi, Fatemeh
Bijad, Majede
Karaman, Ceren
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an electrochemical sensor for the azo dye compound tartrazine (TRT) determination was proposed. A screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was modified by depositing three-dimensional porous reduced graphene oxide decorated with carbon quantum dots and platinum nanoparticles (Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE). The resulting amount of TRT was observed by differential pulse voltammetry. Under optimal conditions, the sensor exhibited two wide linearities ranging from 0.01 to 1.57 μM and 1.57–9.3 μM with the reliability coefficient of determination of 0.991 and 0.992, respectively. The detection limit (LOD) was also estimated to be 7.93 nM. Moreover, the Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE suggested high selectivity in the presence of several interfering agents and azo dye compounds that have a similar structure. Additionally, the Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE revealed superior recovery values of about 96.5–101.6% for candy, 99.7–103.5% for soft drinks, 96.0–101.2% for jelly powder, and 98.0–103.0% for water samples. Furthermore, the fabricated sensor exhibits excellent selectivity, stability, reproducibility, and repeatability, indicating a great perspective in the monitoring of TRT. Therefore, it can be speculated that the proposed electrode could be effectively applied to determine TRT in food samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an electrochemical sensor for the azo dye compound tartrazine (TRT) determination was proposed. A screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was modified by depositing three-dimensional porous reduced graphene oxide decorated with carbon quantum dots and platinum nanoparticles (Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE). The resulting amount of TRT was observed by differential pulse voltammetry. Under optimal conditions, the sensor exhibited two wide linearities ranging from 0.01 to 1.57 μM and 1.57–9.3 μM with the reliability coefficient of determination of 0.991 and 0.992, respectively. The detection limit (LOD) was also estimated to be 7.93 nM. Moreover, the Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE suggested high selectivity in the presence of several interfering agents and azo dye compounds that have a similar structure. Additionally, the Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE revealed superior recovery values of about 96.5–101.6% for candy, 99.7–103.5% for soft drinks, 96.0–101.2% for jelly powder, and 98.0–103.0% for water samples. Furthermore, the fabricated sensor exhibits excellent selectivity, stability, reproducibility, and repeatability, indicating a great perspective in the monitoring of TRT. Therefore, it can be speculated that the proposed electrode could be effectively applied to determine TRT in food samples.

Graphical abstract representing the synthesis of Pt/CQDs@rGO nanocomposite.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Tartrazine, which is a synthetic lemon yellow azo pigment used in food coloring, is employed in a variety of pharmaceutical goods, including vitamins, antacids, cold medicines, and prescription pharmaceuticals, to give them a yellow color for easy identification (Amin et al., 2010). Tartrazine appears to induce the greatest allergic and intolerant responses of all azo dyes, especially in asthmatics and those sensitive to aspirin. Ingestion or cutaneous exposure to a tartrazine-containing chemical might cause symptoms of tartrazine sensitivity. On the other hand, reports show that tartrazine is still utilized in some food products to attract customer attention. Therefore, it is deemed worthwhile to attempt to develop a straightforward method for detecting the presence of TRT in food samples, and the presence and content of tartrazine must be controlled because of their potential harmfulness to human beings and can easily migrate into the environment. Hence, for the monitoring of TRT in food samples via providing fast and sensitive ways, it is crucial to develop low-cost sensors in daily life.

Up to now, in the literature, spectrophotometry (Altιnöz and Toptan, 2002; Sahraei et al., 2013), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Bonan et al., 2013; Garcıa-Falcón and Simal-Gándara, 2005; Hurst et al., 1981; Liao et al., 2012), and capillary electrophoresis (Chou et al., 2002; Huang et al., 2002) methods have been proposed for the determination of tartrazine. Most of these techniques need costly and complicated equipment, time-consuming pretreatment processes, and laborious sample preparations (Amani et al., 2018; Mazloum-Ardakani et al., 2015; Rahimi-Nasrabadi et al., 2017). As a result, a quicker, more straightforward, highly sensitive, and low-cost technique is required for the regular testing of TRT. Electrochemical processes suggest simple, low-cost, sensitive, and rapid determination in analyzing electroactive analytes. Voltammetric techniques, one of the electroanalytical approaches, are important options in food and drug analysis for monitoring the analyte in both qualitative and quantitative terms. The most significant merits of voltammetric methods involve outstanding precision, more straightforward, accuracy, reliability, and low cost. The advancement of these approaches recently has demonstrated that they are viable methods. The SPCE is the most widely used in electrochemical drug and food analysis because of its electrical merits, physical properties, including chemical inertness, wide potential range, and compatibility (Tajik et al., 2021). One of the most crucial parameters in electrocatalytic methods is increasing the electrode's active surface area (Miraki et al., 2019; Shabani-Nooshabadi et al., 2019; Tahernejad-Javazmi et al., 2018). Therefore, materials that can enhance the electrode's active surface area and conductivity are used to modify the electrode surface (Boke et al., 2020; Karaman, 2021; Ozcan et al., 2020).

Nanomaterials have gained substantial attention of different application areas thanks to their peculiar properties (Al Sharabati et al., 2021; Gur et al., 2022; Iverson et al., 2013; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2022). In recent years, amongst the other type of nanostructures, three-dimensional carbon nanomaterials such as graphene oxide have attracted the substantial attention of scientists owing to their outstanding properties, including high surface area, fast electron transportation, superior mechanical strength, and high conductivity (Karaman et al., 2021a, 2021b; Korkmaz et al., 2021). Compared with graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide demonstrates an outstanding conductivity because of the loss of oxygenated surface groups and defects in the basal plane (Karaman et al., 2020). Moreover, the electrochemical properties of reduced graphene oxide can be effectively improved by integration with other functional nanomaterials to enhance electrochemical sensing performance (Karaman et al., 2021a). Several researchers have used metal nanoparticles decorated nanocomposites for the development of electrochemical sensors (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2021; Medetalibeyoglu et al., 2020). Among the metal nanoparticles, platinum nanoparticles have attracted substantial attention for modification of electrodes due to their large specific surface area and high conductivity, enhancing selectivity and sensitivity (Yuan et al., 2011). In addition to Pt nanoparticles, quantum dots-based materials such as molybdenum, carbon, and graphene, which have emerged as a new family of carbon nanomaterials, have become fascinating because of their small size, high sensitivity, excellent electron transfer, comparatively lower cost, easy functionalization, and high electrochemical activity (Dong et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2019; Yuan et al., 2015). Consequently, it can be expected that the rational integration of the merits of rGO, CQDs, and Pt nanoparticles could significantly improve the electrochemical performance of the corresponding hybrid composite, which might serve as a platform for the determination of TRT.

In this work, the novel Pt/CQDs@rGO nanocomposite was synthesized, and then the screen-printed carbon electrode surface was modified by drop-casting its suspension on the electrode surface for the sensitive trace determination of tartrazine. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, it is the first time that the electrocatalytic efficiency and electrochemical sensing performance of Pt/CQDs@rGO were investigated. As a proof-of-concept application, rGO, anchored with Pt nanoparticles and CQDs structure on SCPE (Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE), was used as a sensing alternative for the determination of TRT to reveal the efficiency of the proposed electrochemical sensor. Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE exhibited excellent analytical performance to determine TRT in food samples.

All of the chemicals used were analytical grade and were not purified. Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA) water purification system supplied ultra-pure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm for all experiments. Tartrazine, Allura red, and sunset yellow purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA was used as azo dye compounds, while L-ascorbic acid(99.0%), L-arginine(98.0%), L-cysteine(97.0%), glucose(99.5%), uric acid(99.0%), vitamin B12, vitamin D were used as interfering agents. Citric acid, ethylenediamine, graphene oxide, sodium citrate, hexachloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6), Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), graphite, and potassium permanganate were also obtained from Sigma-Aldrich compound as materials for nanocomposite synthesis.

The synthesized Pt/CQDs@rGO composites were characterized by several spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. SEM micrographs and EDX spectrum of the materials were recorded by ZEISS GeminiSEM 560 at 3.00 kV. The sample X-ray diffraction patterns were acquired by Rigaku smart laboratory diffractometer (operated at 40 kV and 20 mA) with a Cu Kα source at a wavelength of 1.540 Å. Raman spectroscopy was performed by using Raman spectroscopy (WITec alpha 300 M + micro-Raman system with 532 nm laser source). Electrochemical measurements such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), were conducted by AUTO LAB system with PGSTAT128N electrochemical workstation (Metrohm Inc., Switzerland) with a screen-printed carbon electrode (Dropsens Inc.) system.
Please see supplementary information.
Please see supplementary information.

Firstly, the GO dispersion (0.5 mg mL−1, 100 mL) was sonicated for about 2 h before being rapidly agitated in the boiling condition. Afterward, the boiled GO dispersion was added 4 mL of aqueous sodium citrate and 6.0 mL ammonia (solution 1). After that, 1.0 mL CQDs was added to the as-obtained solution and stirred at 300 rpm for 1h. Eventually, 1.0 mL of H2PtCl6 (1000 ppm) solution was poured into solution 1. Then, freshly prepared NaBH4 solution was added drop by drop to the mixture under vigorous stirring at 80 °C over 10 h. The solution color gradually changed during the process and eventually turned black. Finally, the mixture was frozen for two days. As a result, Pt/rGO/CQDs nanocomposites with three-dimensional (3D) architecture were successfully synthesized (Lin et al., 2018).

Candy, soft drink, jelly powder, and water samples were acquired from the local market and were employed for real sample analysis. To begin with, for the preparation of candy and jelly powder, a certain amount of candy and jelly powder was dissolved in hot deionized water, followed by diluted with 0.1 M BR buffer solution at pH = 3.0. Afterward, it was filtered through a 0.45-μm membrane filter to remove the suspended solids and analyzed with DPV in 0.1 M BR buffer (pH = 3.0), and also soft drink samples were employed directly without any pretreatment. The obtained samples were used directly to analyze tartrazine in food products by the standard addition method.

0.5 mg of Pt/CQDs@rGO nanocomposite was dispersed in 1.0 mL deionized water (0.5 mg ml−1 optimized concentration), and the solution was sonicated until a uniform Pt/CQDs@rGO dispersion was obtained. Subsequently, 10.0 μL of Pt/CQDs@rGO suspension (optimized amount) was utilized for modifying the screen-printed carbon electrode. Afterward, to remove unabsorbed nanocomposite on the electrode surface, the electrode was rinsed several times with deionized water, followed by the solvent was removed by an infrared heat lamp. Then, the fabricated electrode was labeled as Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE. All electrodes were kept in a sealed box without fluctuations of temperature and pressure.

Bearing all the aforementioned information in mind and considering the benefits of the 3D reduced graphene oxide-based nanocomposite, Pt/CQDs@rGO was used to fabricate modified electrodes to apply the synergetic effects to increase electrochemical performance. Compared with other electrochemical sensors, the sensors based on rGO-nanocomposites have several advantages for the determination of the desired analyte, including large surface area, rich π-conjugation structure, and outstanding in-plane conductivity, and a unique porous structure of rGO nanosheets and enhanced work function due to crystal texturing of Pt nanoparticles that facilitate the charge transfer at nanocomposite surface. The literature (Gascho et al., 2019; Ghanem et al., 2020; Gong et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2015; Shin et al., 2009; Stankovich et al., 2007) has proven that Pt nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots, and reduced graphene oxide are excellent materials with high conductivity, expansive electrochemical windows, low cost, and good biocompatibility. Therefore, these materials are the best alternative material for the electrochemical detection of tartrazine.

Fig. 1 A represents the powder XRD patterns of the pristine GO, N-CQDs, and Pt/CQDs@rGO samples in the range between 5 and 90°. The characteristic peak of (002) obtained at 11.0°(d002 = 0.85 nm) in the XRD patterns is associated with graphene oxide [23,24]. It can be seen that the characteristic peak (002) shifted to 28.9° in Pt/CQDs/rGO, showing that the reduction of GO is entirely successful. The XRD pattern of CQDs is exhibited in Fig. 1A. A broad diffraction peak at 24.0° is observed, indicating an amorphous carbon phase and partial graphitization of CQDs [25], which are assigned to the presence of large functional groups and graphitization of carbon atoms [26]. The results have reaffirmed that a graphite structure exists in the carbon core of CQDs. After the reduction process, the diffraction peaks are acquired at 46.9°, 55.7°, and 81.6°, corresponding to (111), (200), and (220) planes of the face-centered-cubic (fcc) structure of Pt, respectively [27]. The crystallite sizes of GO, CQDs, and Pt/CQDs@rGO are calculated as 54, 17, and 38 nm by the Scherrer Equation [28]. The large crystallite size of Pt/CQDs@rGO can be assigned to the aggregation of Pt nanoparticles.

Raman spectroscopy has long been utilized to characterize the structural properties of graphitic materials, providing information on defects, graphene layer stacking, and the finite sizes of crystallites parallel and perpendicular to the hexagonal axis, among other things. Raman spectra have been used to characterize GO, Pt/CQDs@rGO to investigate the carbonaceous materials in Fig. 1B (Malard et al., 2009). Graphene oxide exhibits the significant peaks at 1350 and 1580 cm−1 corresponding to D band (the defects and asymmetrical breaking at the graphitic structure arising out of sp3 bonds caused by oxidation) and G band (first-order scattering of the E2g phonon of sp2 C atoms relating to the graphitic hexagon-pinch mode) (Cui et al., 2011; Kudin et al., 2008; Stankovich et al., 2007). The appearance of these two bands is suggestive of rGO's typical characteristics. D and G bands have been detected at 1350 and 1580 cm−1 in the case of GQDs. Similarly, for Pt/N-CQDs@rGO nanocomposite, these bands are formed at 1357 and 1590 cm−1 because of the presence of numerous functional groups, resulting in additional structural defects; these wavenumber values deviate slightly from those of pure graphene oxide. The lesser intensity bands at 2690 and 2925 cm−1 and a low-intensity shoulder at higher wavenumbers can be attributed to the 2D and D + G bands, respectively. ID/IG ratio values of 0.88 and 0.94 have been calculated for GO and Pt/CQDs@rGO, respectively. The rise in this band intensity ratio is due to the formation of a large number of new graphitic domains during the reduction process, which result from sp3 hybridized carbon atoms near the exfoliation's edge and the presence of Pt. The in-plane crystallite size is related to the ratio of the integral intensities of the G and D bands, which was determined using the following equation (Eq. (1)) (Grayfer et al., 2011):

In Table S1, the Raman band positions have been summarized with assignments and calculations. The sizes of crystalline domains are calculated to be approximately 15 and 24 nm for GO and Pt/N-CQDs@rGO.

FE-SEM has been performed to investigate the morphologies of GO, rGO, CQDs, and Pt/CQDs@rGO nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 2 . The wider interspaces of the layer and the thinner layer edges of GO can be seen in Fig. 2A. The morphology structure of GO is shown as a flaky texture indicating its layered microstructure. The SEM image of rGO is oriented as randomly crumpled silk veil waves due to the van der Waal's interactions(Fig. 2B). The SEM image of the Pt/CQDs@rGO exhibits a high density of nonaligned needle-like nanowires(platinum nanowire) with uniform diameters of approximately 80 nm. Also, the image of the as-deposited film clearly depicts randomly distributed Pt nanoparticles between the surface of carbon quantum dots and rGO nanosheets (Fig. 2C and D). The elemental composition and purity analysis of Pt/CQDs@rGO has been investigated by EDX. The EDX investigation exhibits all essential elements such as C, O, Na, Cl, and Pt individually from Pt/CQDs@rGO nanocomposite, as explained in Fig. S1. The percentages of weight are also observed and tabulated in Table S2. Moreover, the mapping of Pt/CQDs@rGO nanocomposite is indicated that the presence of the element of the synthesized nanocomposite, as shown in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic determination of tartrazine using Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electron transfer properties of bare and fabricated electrodes have been observed using CV and EIS techniques using 0.1 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution as a redox probe solution. Heterogeneous electron transfer (HET) rate is a crucial attribute, which is related to peak-to-peak separation (ΔEp) of the anodic and cathodic peaks (Fig. 3 A), to investigate the electrocatalytic efficiency of the sensor material (Alizadeh et al., 2021). A low ΔEp value shows a fast HET rate and better conductivity in sensing applications. Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE exhibited the ΔEp value of 150.1 mV value with larger anodic and cathodic currents compared to bare SPE with ΔEp value of 225.4 mV for Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. The results suggest the fastest HET rate of Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE, which can be attributed to electron transfer ability and electrocatalytic activity due to Pt nanoparticles, rGO, and CQDs's synergic effect with a large surface area and excellent conductivity.

The main factor affecting the electrochemical sensor's sensitivity is the electroactive surface area (EASA) due to the increasing interaction between the analyte and electrode surface. Therefore, the electroactive surface area of the bare and developed electrode is assessed by the Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. (2)) (Mehmandoust et al., 2021a) in 0.1 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− as a redox probe solution. Using the slope of the plot Ipa against v1/2, EASA of Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE is observed to be 0.61 cm−2, which is 1.42 times greater than bare SPCE.

EIS is a superior technique to investigate the conductivity of materials, was observed to study the charge transfer resistance (RCT) value of Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE and bare electrode in Fe(CN)6 3−/4− as redox probe solution after modification. Fig. 3B illustrates the Nyquist plots of the Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE and bare electrode. The Nyquist plot's semicircle diameter is related to the RCT value between the electrode and solution interface. The bare electrode demonstrates a larger semicircle (∼9.7 kΩ) than Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE (∼3.5 kΩ), suggesting the slower electron transfer rate on the electrode surface because of its lower conductivity. Because of the low functional moieties and synergic effects of rGO and CQDs, the EIS measurements have verified the increased conductivity nature of Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE. Therefore, The results have also exhibited that the Pt/CQDs@rGO nanocomposite electrode is a promising electrochemical sensor for TRT determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of pH and scan rate
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value of electrolyte solutions is critical for investigating the electrochemical behavior of analytes, The impact of pH has been studied to achieve the highest current response of the sensor towards TRT by DPV of 10.0 μM TRT in 0.1 B-R buffer with a pH range from 3.0 to 8.0, as shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4A and B exhibits the peak current and peak potential strongly depending on solution pH. The oxidation peak potential of TRT has shifted towards the negative potentials with enhancing pH from 3.0 to 8.0 (Fig. 4A). It exhibits that protons are involved in TRT oxidation. On the other hand, the peak oxidation current of TRT decreased after pH 3.0 to 8.0 (Fig. 4B, blue line); thus, pH 3.0 has been selected as the optimum pH value for further experiments. Fig. 4B (red line) exhibits the plots of the oxidation potential of TRT vs. pH. A decreasing trend can be observed at Epa = −0.0322 pH + 1.0472, and according to the Nernst equation (Mehmandoust et al., 2021b), the m/n (proton number/electron number) ratio has been obtained as 1:2. The results indicated that the number of protons and electrons in the electro-oxidation of TRT are one and two, respectively. The possible electro-oxidation mechanism for TRT at Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE has been represented in Scheme 1 (Chebotarev et al., 2019).

The scan rate is an essential experimental parameter to assess the effects of the adsorbed adsorbent or the reaction on the electrode through diffusion. Therefore, the effect of scan rate on TRT electro-oxidation has been examined to disclose the electrode reaction process in 0.1 M BR (pH 3.0) in the presence of 10.0 μM TRT at different scan rates (10.0–275.0 mV s−1) using Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE (Fig. 5 A). The scan rate effect on the TRT system shows that as the scan rate enhances, the intensity of peak current increases, and the peak potential shifts to higher positive values. The irreversible characteristic of electron transfer processes is shown in the positive change of peak potentials with scan rate. The plot of peak current (Ipa) vs. the square root of scan rate (v1/2) was observed to be linear in the range 10.0–275.0 mVs−1 (Ipa = 11.128 v1/2 +1.13584, (R2 = 0.9915)), showing that the process of TRT at the developed electrode is diffusion-controlled Fig. 5B) (Kunpatee et al., 2019). The slope of the logarithm of peak current vs. the logarithm of scan rates (Fig. 5C) also has proved that the electrode progression is a diffusion-controlled electrode process because the slope of the log Ip against log v is less than 0.5. Fig. 5D offers a linear relationship between Epa and ln v for TRT as presented in the following equation: Epa (V) = 0.0198 ln v + 1.0139 (R2 = 0.9952). In Laviron's theory, for an irreversible electrode reaction, Epa is determined by Eq. (3).

Hence, the number of transferred electrons in the electro-oxidation of TRT is estimated to be 1.73 (≅2). The results have confirmed that two-electrons and one-proton are transferred during electro-oxidation of TRT on Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE surface. Based on the observations using Laviron's theory and slope of Epa vs. pH, a possible electrode reaction mechanism is proposed for TRT, which has involved one proton and two electrons transfer (Scheme 1) (Chebotarev et al., 2019; Gan et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2017). To calculate α, the Tafel plot has been observed between the peak potential and log of current Fig. S3. The charge transfer coefficient has been estimated by using the following equation (Eq. (4)):

where Δ reveals the slope of the Tafel plot, n shows the number of electrons, F stands for Faraday constant, R and T exhibit the universal gas constant, and temperature. Therefore, the α value is calculated to be 0.75.

Chronoamperometric measurements of TRT at Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE have been conducted at 1.0 V for the different concentrations of TRT(100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μM) in B-R buffer at pH 3.0 (Fig. 6 A). For the electrochemical reaction at the mass transport limited condition, the diffusion coefficient of D has been observed by the current versus t−1/2 and Cottrell equation (Eq. (5)) (Fig. 6B):
(5)
I = n F A
D
1 2
C b
π
− 1
2
t
− 1
2
where I reveals current, n represents the number of electrons transferred, F is Faraday's Constant (96,485 C/mol), A shows the electrode area, D represents the diffusion coefficient, Cb stands for the initial concentration, and t is time. From the resulting slope and Cottrell equation, the mean value of the D has been estimated to be 1.35 × 10−5 cm2/s.

The quantitative performance of the fabricated electrode for analysis of TRT has been investigated by DPV at the obtained optimum conditions at Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE surface. As can be seen in Fig. 7 A, the anodic current of TRT enhances linearly with increasing concentration in the ranges of 0.01–1.57 and 1.57–9.3 μM, which can be stated by the following linear regression equation: Ipa = 2.918 CTRT + 0.4134 (R2 = 0.9912) and Ipa = 1.0592 CTRT + 3.8422 (R2 = 0.992) (Fig. 7B). The sensitivity (slope) reduction in the second linear range is most likely due to high concentrations' kinetic limitations (Golabi et al., 2002). The detection limits (LOD) for TRT, given as 3σ/m (Baghayeri et al., 2019), where σ shows the standard deviation of the blank response (n = 10), and m is the slope of the calibration curve, which has been calculated as to be 7.93 nM and curves with R2 ≥ 0.99 are usually considered linear. Some characteristic parameters such as the detection method, linear concentration range, and the detection limit of other reported sensors are tabulated in Table 1 . The remarkable point is that the detection limit obtained using the mentioned method is much less than that obtained by other electrochemical methods. These results have indicated that the suggested Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE could be used as an alternative platform for detecting TRT.

The repeatability and reproducibility of the developed sensor have been also assessed. To test the electrode repeatability, ten experiments have been conducted using the same standard solution of TRT and the same electrode, leading to the response relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.78% (Fig. S4). Moreover, five independently fabricated electrodes are employed with an RSD of 1.37% (Fig. S5), suggesting the fabricated electrode has outstanding repeatability and reproducibility. Additionally, the stability of one Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE has been also examined. After twenty days, the developed electrode that was stored at 4 °C under optimal conditions has offered the current response is about 98.46% of the original value. The excellent long-term stability of the developed electrode makes them fantastic for developing electrochemical sensors. Finally, the reusability of Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE has been observed and it has been revealed that the Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE is not a disposable sensor. It can be utilized at least 11 times by rinsing with 0.1 M BR buffer (pH 3.0).

Sensitive detection of TRT without interference is challenging when applying the DPV method for real sample analysis. Under the optimal conditions, the interference studies have been performed for the TRT determination using a TRT solution mixture with various interfering agents. Fig. 8 displays the DPV of TRT in the presence of a 100-fold concentration of ascorbic acid, l-arginine, l-cysteine, glucose, uric acid, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and a 75-fold concentration of Allura red and sunset yellow. The obtained results exhibit no influence on 1.0 μM TRT electrochemical currents with RSDs below 5%. These results confirm that the fabricated electrode has high selectivity and specificity for TRT detection.

The standard addition procedure has been applied to analyze the target analytes in candy, soft drink, jelly powder, and water samples to validate the method's applicability. The contents have been determined using the standard addition method (Table 2 ). The recoveries and response relative standard deviation are between 96.0 and 103.5% and less than 5%, respectively. The results exhibit the accuracy, precision, and independence of the method from matrix effects. Hence, the results imply the potential of using the fabricated electrode for future practical application as a disposable electrode.

A novel electrochemical sensor based on Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE was developed to directly determine TRT in food samples. The successful synthesis of Pt/CQDs@rGO nanocomposite was confirmed by the various characterization techniques including XRD, Raman, SEM, and EDX. The designed Pt/CQDs@rGO/SPCE exhibited excellent analytical performance with a low detection limit of 7.93 nM and two linear ranges 0.01–1.57 μM and 1.57–9.3 μM for the determination of TRT with outstanding selectivity. Moreover, it can be speculated that the proposed electrode has been successfully applied to detect TRT in food samples with satisfactory recovery and acceptable RSD.
